PREP: Lesson one is safe sleep
Baby sleeps on her back, in her own cot or bassinet in your room, on a firm flat mattress with nothing loose in it. Everything else in this module is negotiable; this is not.
Book a baby first-aid course
Two hours learning infant CPR and choking response, for both of you, is the best-value education you will ever buy. Book it this week.
Antenatal classes fill fast
Go together. The hands-on nappy and bath practice matters most for the parent who currently knows nothing.
Tame the car seat now
Buy it new, fit it rear-facing, and practise the install today. First attempts take forty minutes of swearing, and hospital day is not the time.
Start a midwife question list
A shared phone note of every question as it occurs to you. Sleep-deprived brains forget everything; a running list does not.
Scout the hospital
Learn the route, the parking, and the after-hours entrance. Confidence on the day is mostly logistics done early.
Double every dinner
From tonight, whatever you cook, cook twice and freeze half. Aim for two to three weeks of dinners banked before she arrives.
Choose her doctor
Pick the GP or clinic now and find out how newborn checks and vaccinations get booked where you live.
Do the paperwork sprint
Leave forms, birth registration rules, any government benefits. Deadlines vary by country and sneak up; lodge what you can this week.
Bags packed by 36 weeks
Three bags: mum, baby, you. The full list is in the Checklists tab. Snacks for labour go in yours; you are the catering department.
Breastfeeding is a learned skill
For both mother and baby, not an instinct. A feeding class or lactation consultant session before birth pays for itself many times over.
Your feeding job, defined
Everything around the feed: fetch water, burp her, change the nappy, settle her back down. That is not helping. That is half the job.
Know the newborn rhythm
She will feed eight to twelve times a day, roughly every two to three hours, around the clock. Plan the first weeks around that, not the reverse.
Buy fewer clothes
Newborn sizes last weeks. Zip suits beat buttons at 3am, every single time, forever.
Ignore most of the shop
Truly needed: a safe place to sleep, car seat, nappies, clothes, feeding gear, a carrier or pram. Almost everything else can wait until you know her.
Set up the change station
Everything within one arm's reach, because you can never step away from a baby on a raised surface. Not for one second, not ever.
Practise the swaddle
White noise, dim light and a snug wrap soothe newborns because they feel like the womb. Practise on a teddy now; laugh at yourself; get good.
Design the nights in daylight
Agree who takes which shift and how the working parent contributes without falling asleep in meetings. Write it down. Renegotiate weekly.
Set the visitor policy together
Who, when, how long, and anyone unwell stays home. You present a united front, and you play doorman.
Learn hunger cues before crying
Stirring, mouth opening, head turning and rooting, hands to mouth. Crying is the last stop on the hunger train, not the first.
Read up on day-one decisions
Vitamin K and newborn screening happen in the first days. Read about them now so the consent conversation is not your first exposure.
Third-trimester support brief
She needs iron-rich food, water, and side-sleeping. You supply the cooking, the hydration reminders, and take over the household admin.
Rehearse a bath and a nappy
On a doll at class if you can. It sounds silly. It deletes about eighty percent of day-one terror.
Stock the medicine drawer
A good baby thermometer, saline drops, a nasal aspirator, and infant paracetamol for later months. Ask the pharmacist to walk you through each one.
The layers rule
Babies regulate temperature poorly. Dress her in one more layer than you are comfortable in, and check warmth on her chest, never her hands.
Cord care is easy
Keep the stump clean and dry, fold the nappy below it, and it drops off within one to three weeks. No swimming lessons required.
Plan the day-of comms
Who calls whom, in what order, and what the birth certificate deadline is where you live. Decide before the adrenaline does.
Build mum's recovery corner
Water bottle, snacks, charger, nipple cream, maternity pads, all within reach of the feeding chair. She will live there for a while.
Meet the witching hour
Many babies cry hardest from late afternoon to late evening in the early months. Given your commute, that is your shift: walk in, wash hands, take her.
Your relationship is infrastructure
Book one easy date before the birth, and agree a weekly fifteen-minute check-in on each other afterwards. Put it in the calendar now.
Learn the phrase cluster feeding
Evening marathons where she feeds on and off for hours. Normal, temporary, and it builds milk supply. It is not a sign something is wrong.
Set the photo rules
A shared private album, and an agreed social media policy for her face, before the grandparents decide it for you.
Wash the first wardrobe
First batch of clothes and bedding in gentle detergent, and pack a going-home outfit. Plus a spare. Always a spare.
Know the signs of labour
Regular strengthening contractions, waters breaking, a show. Know exactly at what point your hospital wants a phone call.
Sort the home front
Pets, plants, a spare key with someone reliable, mail. Assign an on-call person for anything domestic while you are at the hospital.
Buy and learn a baby carrier
It gives your wife her hands back and it is the working parent's superpower for evening settling. Practise before you need it.
Watch each other's heads
Perinatal anxiety and depression affect mothers and fathers both. Learn the signs (Library, course six) so you can watch out for each other.
Turn the car into a depot
Pram in the boot, muslins, spare nappies, a charger. The car quietly becomes a second nursery; stock it like one.
Agree the sleep-in rule
Whoever had the worse night gets the morning lie-in, and the question are-you-okay always requires a real answer. House rules; write them down.
Pack for skin-to-skin
The first hour of cuddles on bare chests helps feeding and bonding, and it is for you too. Pack a shirt that opens at the front.
Gently re-train the grandparents
Safe sleep advice has changed since the eighties. The cot rules outrank nostalgia, kindly but firmly, and you deliver that message so mum does not have to.
Final-month protocol
Fuel above half, phone charged, work informed, bag by the door. Boring readiness is what calm looks like.
Ask about hand-expressing
Midwives teach expressing colostrum from around 36 to 37 weeks where appropriate. Ask if it suits your pregnancy first; it is gold if latching is tricky early on.
Draft your village list
Who does food, who does laundry, who answers a 2am phone call. When people say let-me-know-if-you-need-anything, assign a job from the list.
The whole syllabus in one line
A fed baby, a safe cot, and two parents watching out for each other. Everything else you are allowed to learn as you go.

BANDS.name: First fortnight
tips: Guard the bubble
Today's entire job: feed, sleep, skin-to-skin, repeat. Visitors, tidying and the outside world can all wait. You are the doorman and quartermaster.
Nappy maths, week one
Roughly one wet nappy per day of age until day five, then five or six heavy ones daily. It is the simplest sign she is getting enough milk.
Her stomach is a cherry
Day-one stomach capacity is tiny, so tiny frequent feeds are the design, not a malfunction. It grows to egg-sized within a couple of weeks.
The poo timeline
Black and tarry, then green-brown, then mustard-yellow and seedy by about day five if breastfeeding. All normal, all good news.
Day three to five: milk and tears
Milk usually comes in now, often with engorgement and a hormone crash that brings tears for mum. Warm showers, frequent feeds, extra kindness, zero commentary.
Weight dips, then returns
Losing up to about seven to ten percent of birth weight early on is normal; she should be back to it around two weeks. The midwife tracks this so you do not have to.
Wake her to feed for now
If she sleeps longer than three to four hours in these first weeks, wake her gently for a feed until weight gain is established. Sleepy jaundiced babies especially.
Watch the yellow
Jaundice from day two or three is common and usually fades. Yellowing that deepens or spreads, a very sleepy baby, or poor feeding needs a same-day review.
Burping, the honest version
Upright on the shoulder, firm rhythmic pats, five minutes maximum. Some babies burp instantly, some never do, both are fine.
Sleep in watches
Sleep-when-the-baby-sleeps is half right. The real system: one parent sleeps properly while the other holds the fort, in shifts, like crew on a boat.
Mum's red flags are yours to watch
Soaking a pad an hour, large clots, fever, severe headache or vision changes, or a painful red calf: call the midwife or emergency services immediately.
Newborns are noisy sleepers
Grunts, squeaks, irregular breathing with short pauses: normal. Working hard to breathe, grunting with every breath, or blue lips: get help now.
You cannot spoil a newborn
Every cuddle answered builds security, not habits. The fourth trimester has one rule: respond.
Take the photo
The three of you, today, however wrecked you look. It is the one you will frame.

BANDS.name: Weeks 2 to 6
tips: Growth spurts are a thing
Around two to three weeks and again near six: a day or two of near-constant feeding and grizzle. Supply catches up. It is not a milk shortage.
Watch the wake window
She can handle roughly 45 to 75 minutes awake, feed included. Overtired babies fight sleep harder, so watch the clock as closely as the yawns.
Start tummy time
A few minutes on your chest or a mat, a few times a day, always awake and watched. It builds the neck that later rolls, sits and crawls.
The crying peak is coming
Crying peaks around weeks six to eight, often for no findable reason. The ladder: feed, nappy, burp, swaddle, side-hold and sway, shush, walk. Then start again.
The five-minute rule
If crying ever brings a surge of frustration, put her safely in the cot and leave the room for five minutes. A crying baby in a cot is safe. No exceptions, no shame.
The six-week check is for mum too
Bleeding, pain, continence and mood all belong in that appointment. Encourage complete honesty; nothing on that list is trivial or embarrassing.
Blues fade, PND does not
Baby blues pass within about two weeks. Persistent low mood, anxiety, rage or numbness, in either of you, deserves a GP conversation. Common, treatable, not a verdict.
Anchor jobs for the office parent
Back at work, own the fixtures: the bath, the first night shift, or the dawn change. The day should have your fingerprints on both ends.
Bathing is overrated
Every two to three days is plenty; top and tail the face, neck folds, hands and bottom in between. Milk hides in neck creases; you will smell it first.
First smiles, around six weeks
Wind-smiles come earlier. You will claim every one of them regardless, as is your right.
Book the first vaccinations
They start around six to eight weeks in most countries. Plan a quiet cuddly day after; mild grizzles and a slightly raised temperature are common.
The best walking-in question
Not what's-for-dinner. Try: what can I take off you right now? Her day was a thousand small decisions made alone.

BANDS.name: Weeks 6 to 12
tips: A rhythm is emerging
Feed, play, sleep on a rough two-to-three-hour loop. Follow her pattern for a week and write it down before you try to shape it.
Teach day versus night
Bright, chatty, normal-volume days; dark, dull, whispered nights. The same feed gets a completely different lighting design.
Coos are conversation
Answer her sounds, pause, let her reply. This turn-taking is literally how language begins, and she prefers your voices to any toy.
Rolling ends swaddling
At the first sign of rolling, the swaddle retires that day and she moves to an arms-free baby sleeping bag.
The block-lap settle
Rough evening? The pram around the block or the carrier plus a podcast is a legitimate settling strategy, mild exercise, and paternity in its natural habitat.
Sleeping through, redefined
At this age it means one five-hour stretch, usually the first of the night. Protect that stretch for whichever parent needs it most.
Mothers' group is oxygen
Parent groups and baby classes exist for the adults' sanity more than the babies' development. Encourage them like her wellbeing depends on it, because it partly does.
She found her hands
Staring at them, jamming them in her mouth. Around now, hand-sucking is not always hunger; check the clock as well as the cues.
Weekend protocol
Each parent gets one guilt-free block of hours completely off duty. Calendar it like a meeting neither of you may move.
Read to her daily
Anything counts, even reading your work aloud. It is about voice, rhythm and closeness, not plot.
Keep the bottle warm
If feeding is going well, one expressed-milk bottle a day from around six to eight weeks keeps the skill alive and buys mum freedom. Ideal ownership: yours.
You are the show
By eight weeks she tracks you across the room and prefers faces to everything else. Ham it up.

BANDS.name: Months 3 to 6
tips: The four-month upgrade
The famous regression is a permanent change: she now cycles through light sleep like an adult. Consistent settling and well-timed naps carry you through the bumpy weeks.
Windows stretch
Awake time grows to roughly 1.5 to 2.5 hours and most babies settle near three to four naps. Build the day around the reliable morning nap.
Rolling changes the rules
From now, never leave her unattended on any raised surface, and keep a hand on her during changes. The floor is the only safe unattended venue.
Drool is not always teeth
Average first tooth is around six months but three to fourteen is normal. Chilled teething rings beat gels; ask a pharmacist before using anything else.
Solids countdown
Readiness looks like: sitting with support, strong head control, real interest in your food, and losing the tongue-thrust reflex. Around six months, never before four.
Find the laugh button
Usually raspberries on the tummy. Once located, press relentlessly.
One cot nap a day
If naps are chaos, aim for at least one in the cot; the rest can be pram, carrier or car. Practice beats perfection.
Use it or lose it
Bottles get refused after a few dropped weeks. Little and often keeps the skill, and a cup becomes an option from six months.
Crawl your own house
Get on hands and knees and survey the living room: cords, coins, corners, tippy furniture. Fix the worst five hazards this weekend.
The half-year board meeting
Fifteen minutes as a couple: what works, what is brutal, who needs more sleep, exercise or friends. One agenda item each. Wine permitted.
Babble back
Coos become ba, ga, ma strings. Echo her, exaggerate your mouth shapes, and narrate everything you touch.
Start the childcare homework
If a return to work near twelve months is likely, good centres often carry long waitlists. Visiting a few now costs nothing and buys options.

BANDS.name: Months 6 to 9
tips: Iron first
First foods should be iron-rich: fortified baby cereal, minced meat, lentils, egg. Milk remains the main food until twelve months; solids are practice and top-up.
Allergens early, one at a time
Peanut butter, egg, dairy, wheat, fish: introduce early, in the morning, a few days apart. Early introduction reduces allergy risk; delaying does not.
Gagging versus choking
Gagging is loud, red-faced, normal learning. Choking is silent. Rewatch the choking section of your first-aid course tonight, both of you.
The forbidden list
No honey before twelve months, no whole nuts, no added salt or sugar, and cow's milk only in cooking, not as the main drink. Water starts with meals now.
Mess is the curriculum
Squishing, smearing and dropping food is how texture and cause-and-effect are learned. A cheap shower curtain under the high chair saves your floor and sanity.
Her order, her timing
Sitting, creeping, crawling, in any order; some babies skip crawling entirely. Compare her only to herself last month.
Clinging is a compliment
Separation anxiety around seven to nine months means attachment is working. Brief cheerful goodbyes beat sneaking out, every time.
The two-nap shape
By eight or nine months most days settle into two naps, roughly mid-morning and early afternoon. When day sleep firms up, nights tend to follow.
Danger audit, round two
She is mobile. Locks on chemical cupboards, medicines up high, cords tied, straps on any furniture that could tip.
Peekaboo is neuroscience
She is learning that hidden things still exist. It is also the cheapest toy you will ever own.
Change only what is breaking you
Feeding to sleep, rocking, dummy runs: all normal. If a habit is genuinely breaking you, pick one gentle change and hold it for two full weeks before judging.
Take the date night
A trusted sitter, a bottle or an early bedtime, and out the door. It feels wrong for ten minutes and wonderful for the rest.

BANDS.name: Months 9 to 12
tips: Cruising before walking
Furniture-surfing comes first; walking lands anywhere from nine to eighteen months. Bare feet are best for learning.
Point at everything
Pointing, waving and clapping around nine to twelve months are major communication milestones. Mention it to your GP or nurse if none have appeared by twelve months.
She eats what you eat
Three meals plus snacks, family food chopped small, minus salt, honey and whole nuts. Congratulations, you now cook for three.
Dada comes first
Usually because the sound is easier, nothing personal to mum. Keep narrating, keep reading, keep pausing for her turn.
Redirect beats lecture
No starts working in both directions. She is testing physics and boundaries, not your authority; distraction is the senior tool.
The one-year sleep shape
Usually two naps drifting toward one, and eleven to twelve hours overnight. Milestone weeks still wobble it; hold the routine and it passes.
Plan the childcare on-ramp
If mum returns to work near twelve months: orientation visits four to six weeks early, and budget for a month of extra bugs once she starts. Agree whose diary flexes.
Weaning is mum's call
Whenever it happens, gradual is kindest to everyone. From twelve months, full-cream cow's milk can step in as a drink.
Book the twelve-month check
Vaccinations plus a general once-over. Bring the worry list, however small the items feel; that is what the appointment is for.
Shoes can wait
Buy them only when she walks outdoors: soft, flexible, properly fitted. Until then, grippy socks and bare feet.
The party is for the adults
She will not remember the first birthday; you will. Recreate one photo from her first week and watch the room go quiet.
Look how far you have come
Reread day one of this brief. You knew nothing. You are now the world experts on one small person. Next: toddlerhood, where the rules change again.

MODS.tag: The fourth trimester, crying, soothing and what she can perceive

lessons.b:
Human babies arrive earlier in their development than almost any other mammal, so the first three months are best treated as a continuation of pregnancy on the outside. She is not being difficult; she is adjusting to gravity, light, hunger and air all at once.

  • Recreating the womb (snug wrapping, motion, white noise, closeness) is soothing, not spoiling
  • You cannot create bad habits in the first three months
  • Expect her to want to be held most of the time; a carrier makes that survivable

lessons.t: What she can see, hear and smell
lessons.b:
At birth she sees about 20 to 30 centimetres, exactly the distance to a feeding parent's face, and prefers faces and high-contrast patterns to everything else. She already knows both your voices from the womb and can smell her mother from across the room.

  • Talk and sing constantly; your voices are her favourite thing on earth
  • Black-and-white patterns fascinate her in the early weeks
  • By 8 weeks she tracks moving faces; by 3 months she prefers yours to a stranger's

lessons.t: Why babies cry, and the soothing ladder
lessons.b:
Crying is her only broadcast channel and it peaks around weeks 6 to 8, sometimes for no findable reason at all. Work a consistent ladder rather than panicking through random fixes.

  • Check in order: hunger, nappy, wind, tired, too hot or cold, wants contact
  • Then soothe: snug wrap, hold on the side or stomach across your forearm, gentle sway, rhythmic shushing, something to suck, a walk outside
  • Some evening crying with no cause is normal and passes; it is not your failure
If crying ever brings a surge of anger, put her safely in the cot and leave the room for five minutes. Never, ever shake a baby.

Newborns come with startling built-in behaviour that alarms unbriefed parents.

  • The startle (Moro) reflex flings arms wide at noises or movement; swaddling calms it
  • Sneezing, hiccups, crossed eyes, quivering chins and irregular breathing are all normal
  • Newborn girls can have mini-periods and swollen breasts from maternal hormones in week one; harmless and brief
  • Baby acne and flaky skin peak around weeks 3 to 6 and clear on their own

Expect sudden 24 to 48 hour periods of extra feeding, clinginess and worse sleep around 2 to 3 weeks, 6 weeks, 3 months and 6 months. Supply and sanity both catch up.

  • Feed on demand through them; they build milk supply
  • They often end with a new skill appearing
  • Do not schedule anything ambitious during one

MODS.tag: Breastfeeding, the support role, expressing and bottles

lessons.b:
Milk works on supply and demand: the more she feeds, the more is made. Frequent feeding in the early weeks is the system calibrating, not failing. Expect 8 to 12 feeds per 24 hours at first, 20 to 40 minutes each.

  • Colostrum (days 1 to 3) is tiny in volume and exactly right
  • Milk comes in around day 3 to 5, often with engorgement
  • Feed on demand, not the clock, in the early months

lessons.t: Latch: the thing that makes or breaks it
lessons.b:
Most pain and supply problems trace back to a shallow latch. A good latch is deep and asymmetric: chin buried in the breast, mouth wide, more areola covered below the nipple than above, and rhythmic swallowing you can hear.

  • Persistent pain beyond the first 30 seconds is a signal, not a toll to be paid
  • Ask for help early: midwife, lactation consultant, breastfeeding association helpline
  • Fixing a latch in week one prevents months of trouble

Breasts have no gauge, so use the outputs.

  • Week one: roughly one wet nappy per day of age, then 5 to 6 heavy ones daily
  • Regular yellow poos in the early weeks
  • Back to birth weight by about two weeks, then steady gain the nurse tracks
  • Generally settled between some feeds, alert when awake
Cluster feeding in the evenings looks like insatiable hunger. It is normal, temporary and supply-building.

The non-feeding parent controls most of what determines whether breastfeeding survives: rest, food, water, morale and airspace defence.

  • Bring water and food to every feed before being asked
  • Own burping, the nappy after, and resettling
  • Guard her from visitors' opinions about feeding; your line is: this is what we have decided
  • Learn the pump and the milk-storage rules so a bottle break is always possible

From around 6 weeks (once feeding is established), one expressed bottle a day keeps bottle skills alive and gives mum freedom. Commonly cited storage rules: about 4 hours at room temperature, up to 4 days in the back of the fridge, and around 6 months frozen; national guidance differs, so confirm the exact numbers with your midwife.

  • Label everything with date and time
  • Warm milk in warm water, never a microwave (hot spots)
  • Paced bottle feeding (upright, horizontal bottle, breaks) suits breastfed babies
  • Formula, if it is ever needed, is a safe and complete food; fed is the objective

Small milky spills (possets) after feeds are laundry, not illness. Wind peaks in the first three months as her gut matures.

  • Burp mid-feed and after: upright on the shoulder, firm pats, five minutes max
  • Keep her upright 15 to 20 minutes after feeds if spilly
  • See the GP for: projectile vomiting, green vomit, poor weight gain, or misery with feeds

MODS.tag: Safe sleep rules, rhythms, wake windows and the regressions

lessons.b:
These rules cut the risk of sudden infant death dramatically and outrank every grandparent, blog and gadget.

  • On her back for every sleep, day and night
  • In her own cot or bassinet, in your room, ideally for the first 6 to 12 months
  • Firm, flat mattress; no pillows, toys, bumpers, weighted items or loose blankets
  • Use a safe sleeping bag or firmly tucked blanket, feet at the foot of the cot
  • Smoke-free house and car; avoid overheating; keep her head uncovered
  • Never sleep with her on a sofa or armchair, and know the risks before any bed-sharing

Sixteen-ish hours a day, in fragments of 2 to 4 hours, distributed with total contempt for your preferences. Day-night confusion is standard issue for the first weeks.

  • Days: bright, noisy, social feeds. Nights: dark, dull, whispered, businesslike
  • Newborn wake windows are only 45 to 75 minutes including the feed
  • Watch tired signs: glazed stare, jerky movements, grizzling, ear-pulling

• 0 to 6 weeks: 45 to 75 minutes
  • 6 to 12 weeks: 60 to 90 minutes
  • 3 to 4 months: 75 minutes to 2 hours
  • 5 to 7 months: 2 to 3 hours
  • 8 to 12 months: 2.5 to 4 hours

These are rough guides, not laws. An overtired baby is harder to settle than an undertired one, so when in doubt, try sleep a little earlier.

A snug swaddle (arms in, hips loose) tames the startle reflex in the early weeks. The moment she shows any sign of rolling, swaddling stops permanently and she moves to an arms-free sleeping bag.

  • A short, boring, identical wind-down (feed, change, wrap or bag, dim room, white noise) becomes a powerful sleep cue within weeks
  • Put her down drowsy-but-awake when you can; abandon it cheerfully when you cannot
  • Hands-on settling in the cot (patting, shushing) is a middle path between rocking and leaving

lessons.t: The four-month change and other regressions
lessons.b:
Around 4 months her sleep architecture permanently matures: more light sleep, more wakings between cycles. Bumps also commonly appear around 8 to 10 months and 12 months alongside big motor and separation-anxiety leaps.

  • Hold the routine steady; regressions pass in 2 to 6 weeks
  • Good nap timing is the strongest lever on nights
  • Whatever she falls asleep with (feeding, rocking, dummy) she may look for between cycles; change it only if it is genuinely breaking you, and gently

Design the nights like a roster, not a competition in martyrdom.

  • Split the night into watches, or alternate full nights on the weekend
  • The office parent can own the first waking or the dawn shift and still function at work
  • If mum feeds, you handle the change and resettle so the feed is the whole of her job
  • The worse-night parent gets the lie-in; renegotiate weekly as her sleep changes

MODS.tag: The unglamorous core skills, mastered in a week

lessons.b:
You will do roughly 2,500 of these in year one; by day ten you will do them half-asleep.

  • Everything in arm's reach before you start; one hand on her always on a raised surface
  • For girls, wipe front to back, and there is no need to clean inside the labia
  • Air time between changes is the best nappy-rash prevention; barrier cream at the first hint of redness
  • Fold the nappy below the cord stump until it drops off

Nappies are your main data feed on intake and health.

  • Week one: meconium (black tar) to green-brown to mustard yellow
  • Breastfed poo is yellow, seedy and loose, anywhere from every feed to once in several days after week six; both extremes can be normal
  • See a doctor promptly for: white or clay-coloured poo, red blood, black poo after week one, or a dry-nappy day

Two or three baths a week is plenty; daily baths dry her skin. Top and tail (face, neck folds, hands, bottom) with cotton wool and warm water in between.

  • Water around 37 to 38 degrees; test with your wrist or elbow, or use a bath thermometer
  • A few centimetres of water, one forearm behind her head and shoulders, never let go
  • Never leave her in water unattended for any reason, not even to grab a towel
  • Plain water is fine for weeks; fragrance-free wash later if you like

• Nails: file or use baby scissors after a bath when soft, or during sleep; mittens are a losing battle
  • Cradle cap (yellow crusty scalp): soften with baby oil, brush gently, harmless
  • Dry patches: fragrance-free moisturiser; persistent angry red patches: ask the GP about eczema
  • Sun: under 6 months lives in shade, hats and covering clothes rather than sunscreen

MODS.tag: Fever rules, the check-up rhythm and when to act

lessons.b:
Fever is a symptom, not an illness, but age changes its meaning entirely.

  • Under 3 months: a temperature of 38.0 or higher is a same-day medical review, every time, even if she seems well
  • 3 to 6 months: 39 or higher, or any fever with worrying behaviour, gets seen
  • Any age: how she looks and behaves matters more than the number alone
  • Buy a decent digital thermometer and learn it before you need it at 2am

Expect a schedule of nurse or GP checks through the first year covering weight, development and parental sanity, plus vaccinations typically beginning around 6 to 8 weeks with boosters across the year. Exact schedules vary by country, so get the official one from your GP or child health nurse and load every date into both your calendars.

  • The office parent attends whichever appointments physically possible; the rest get a debrief call
  • Bring the running worry list to every appointment
  • Post-vaccination grizzles and a mildly raised temperature for 24 to 48 hours are common

lessons.t: Colds, snot and the first illness
lessons.b:
Once she meets other children she will average 6 to 12 colds a year, which is normal immune training and still horrible at 3am.

  • Saline drops then a nasal aspirator before feeds and sleep
  • Keep feeds and fluids up; watch nappy output as your dehydration gauge
  • No cough or cold medicines for babies; ask the pharmacist about anything else
  • She cannot blow her nose until roughly age 2. You are the nose

Parents detect wrong before they can articulate it, and clinicians take parental instinct seriously. If she is not herself in a way you cannot name, that alone justifies a call or a visit.

  • Keep the after-hours health line and nearest emergency department saved in both phones
  • Memorise the Red flags tab; review it once a month
  • No good doctor is annoyed by a well baby brought in by worried parents

MODS.t: Mum's recovery, and both your heads
MODS.tag: The patient who also just started the hardest job

lessons.b:
Regardless of delivery type, expect around 6 weeks of significant physical recovery and months of gradual return: bleeding (lochia) for 2 to 6 weeks, afterpains, exhaustion, and either perineal healing or major abdominal surgery recovery after a caesarean.

  • Her only jobs for the first fortnight: feed the baby, rest, heal, eat
  • Your jobs: everything else, plus enforcement of the above
  • After a caesarean: no lifting heavier than the baby and no driving until cleared

• Soaking a maternity pad in an hour, or clots larger than a 50-cent coin
  • Fever, foul-smelling discharge, or a wound that is hot, red or oozing
  • Severe headache, vision changes, or sudden swelling of face and hands
  • A painful, red, swollen calf, or breathlessness and chest pain (call an ambulance)
  • Burning urination, or inability to pass urine

Any of these: midwife or emergency care immediately. You watch for them because she will be too busy and too tired to.

lessons.t: Baby blues, PND and the father's version
lessons.b:
Around 80 percent of mothers get weepy, overwhelmed baby blues from about day 3 to 10; they pass. Postnatal depression and anxiety are different: persistent low mood, dread, rage, numbness, intrusive thoughts or inability to sleep even when the baby does, any time in the first year. Roughly one in seven mothers and one in ten fathers are affected.

  • Agree now: either of you can call it in the other, kindly, and it triggers a GP visit, not a debate
  • Treatment works; suffering through it quietly helps no one, least of all the baby
  • Any thought of self-harm or harming the baby is a call-someone-today emergency, and it is an illness talking, not character

The first year strains every couple: sleep debt, a scorekeeping temptation, and roughly zero unstructured time together. The couples who fare best treat it as a logistics problem, not a love problem.

  • Weekly 15-minute check-in: how are you actually, what was hard, what do you need next week
  • Divide domains, not tasks: one of you owns bath-and-bedtime, one owns laundry-and-kitchen, whole and completely
  • Say the appreciation out loud; assume goodwill; both of you are doing more than the other can see
  • Intimacy returns on its own timetable, after clearance and after readiness; patience and honesty beat pressure

lessons.t: Her year at home, made sustainable
lessons.b:
Twelve months of leave is a marathon of love and monotony. The at-home parent's mental health runs on adult contact, movement and identity, not just baby care.

  • A mothers' group or parent group in the first months is the single best predictor of a sane year; treat joining one as mandatory admin
  • One weekday thing that is hers alone (class, gym, coffee, walk with a friend), guarded like a work meeting
  • Your standing weekday check-in call or message at a set time, so the day has an adult waypoint
  • She gets solo leave-the-house time every weekend, hours not minutes, without a shopping list attached

MODS.tag: Built for your actual arrangements: 12 months leave, 3 weeks + 1 WFH, then 4 office days and a 45-minute commute

lessons.b:
Your three weeks of leave are not holidays and not spectating; they are the apprenticeship that determines whether you are a competent co-parent or a permanent assistant.

  • Own outright from day one: nappies, baths, settling, burping, laundry, food, visitors, admin
  • Do everything solo at least several times while backup is in the next room
  • Nights in shifts from night one; you do not need to lactate to resettle a baby
  • Target for day 21: you can run a feed-adjacent cycle (change, bottle if expressing, settle) start to finish, alone, calmly

lessons.t: Week 4 at home: the dress rehearsal
lessons.b:
Your WFH week is the transition test. Work the job, but structure the day the way the office weeks will need to run.

  • Take the 7am shift before logging on, and the bath-and-bedtime after logging off
  • Cover one full solo block (2 to 3 hours) so mum leaves the house alone; treat it as a drill
  • Set the standing daily check-in time you will keep from the office
  • Friday: run the back-to-work handover checklist in the Lists tab together

With a 45-minute commute and 4 office days, you cannot be present at 3pm; you can absolutely own both ends of the day. Babies attach to whoever does the repeated care, and bookends count double.

  • Morning bookend: first nappy and dress her, or the dawn feed shift, before you leave
  • Evening bookend: walk in, wash hands, take the baby immediately, no decompression lap. The evening witching hour is your shift and mum clocks off the moment you arrive
  • You own bath and bedtime on office days; it becomes your ritual, not your favour to her
  • The commute is your study hall: this brief, a parenting audiobook, or calls to your own parents. Also, occasionally, silence; that is allowed

Physical tasks are easy to split; the invisible load (tracking feeds, sizes, appointments, what needs buying) is what actually buries the at-home parent.

  • Saturday and Sunday mornings: you solo the baby, she sleeps or leaves the house
  • Own whole systems: you are the person who tracks nappy stock, books vaccinations, and knows the next clothing size, without being asked
  • Shared calendar for every appointment; shared note for the running lists
  • Cook or organise the week's dinners on Sunday; the 5pm dinner question should never land on the person holding a baby

lessons.t: Money and admin while you are at it
lessons.b:
One income plus a new dependant deserves the same discipline as any facility you would run at work.

  • Rebuild the budget for the leave year now, not month three
  • Sort life insurance, wills and guardianship nominations before the birth; it is a one-evening job
  • Lodge every government registration and benefit claim early; check your and her employer's parental policies for anything unclaimed
  • If childcare is likely at month 12, waitlists open now and deposits are cheaper than lost options

MODS.tag: Solids, milestones, teeth, proofing and the childcare question

lessons.b:
Around 6 months (never before 4), once she can sit supported, hold her head steady, shows real interest and has lost the tongue-thrust reflex. Milk remains the main food until 12 months.

  • Prioritise iron: fortified cereal, minced red meat, lentils, egg
  • Introduce common allergens (peanut, egg, dairy, wheat, fish) early, one at a time, mornings
  • Purees, mashed food, finger food or a mix: all valid roads to the same place
  • Never: honey before 12 months, whole nuts, added salt or sugar
  • Offer water in a cup with meals; expect most of it on the floor

Ranges are wide and she will be early on some, late on others. Rough shape: rolling 3 to 6 months, sitting 6 to 9, crawling 7 to 10 (or never, legitimately), pulling to stand 9 to 12, first steps 9 to 18, babble from 4 to 6, first words around 12.

  • Compare her to herself last month, not to the mothers' group
  • Worth raising with the nurse or GP: no eye contact or social smiles by 3 months, not responding to sounds or voices, no babble by 9 months, no pointing or waving by 12, or losing skills she had
  • The check-up schedule exists precisely to catch these; attend them all

First tooth averages around 6 months (range 3 to 14); the full set lands by about age 3. Real teething causes drool, chewing, red cheeks and grizzle for a few days per tooth.

  • Chilled (not frozen) teething rings, a cold wet flannel, and cuddles do most of the work
  • Pain relief appropriate to age if she is miserable; ask the pharmacist about anything else first
  • Teething does not cause high fever or diarrhoea; those get assessed on their own merits
  • Brush from tooth one: soft brush, rice-grain smear of toothpaste, twice daily

Proof one stage ahead of her current skills, because new skills arrive overnight and unannounced.

  • By rolling (3 to 4 months): nothing unattended on raised surfaces, cot rules re-audited
  • By sitting and grabbing (6 months): cords up, small objects off the floor (toilet-roll-tube test: if it fits through, it is a choking hazard), hot drinks out of reach forever
  • By crawling (7 to 10 months): gates on stairs, locks on chemical and medicine cupboards, power points covered, furniture that could tip strapped to the wall
  • By cruising (9 to 12): tablecloths gone, TV secured, blind cords cleated high

No program, app or flashcard beats an adult who talks, sings, reads and plays. Her brain builds itself on serve-and-return: she babbles or points, you respond, repeat ten thousand times.

  • Read daily from birth; narrate the boring things you do
  • Floor time beats container time (bouncers, walkers) for motor development
  • Screens: essentially none before 18 months except video calls with family, per most national guidance
  • Boredom is fine; watching you unpack the dishwasher is, to her, prestige television

lessons.t: The childcare decision, ahead of time
lessons.b:
If mum returns to work near month 12, the decision tree runs: long day care, family day care, a nanny or nanny-share, grandparents, or a mix. Quality centres often carry waitlists of 6 to 12 months in cities, so scouting at month 4 to 6 is not keen, it is on time.

  • Visit at least three options; watch how staff talk to the babies, not the brochure
  • Ask about ratios, staff turnover, settling policies and what a hard day looks like
  • Budget for the first month back to be chaos: orientation visits, extra illness, and feelings, all completely normal
  • Whose diary flexes for sick days is a decision to make in advance, not at 6am on the day

items: Hospital bags packed and by the door
Car seat installed and checked
Route, parking and after-hours entrance known
Freezer holds 2+ weeks of dinners
Phone list ready: who calls whom on the day
Pets, plants and mail have an on-call person
Spare house key with someone reliable
Leave paperwork and registrations lodged
First batch of baby clothes and bedding washed
Baby first-aid course booked or done
Maternity pads (lots) and big comfortable underwear
Front-opening nighties or shirts for skin-to-skin and feeding
Toiletries, lip balm, hair ties
Nipple cream and breast pads
Going-home outfit (roughly 6-month-pregnancy sized)
Long phone charging cable
Snacks and an empty water bottle
Any medications, and the pregnancy record or app details
6 zip suits and 6 singlets (newborn and 0-3 sizes)
Hat and socks or booties
Newborn nappies and wipes or cotton wool
3 or 4 muslin wraps
A light blanket
Going-home outfit, plus a spare
A spare spare outfit (trust the brief)
Snacks, then more snacks, and a water bottle
Change of clothes and basic toiletries
Front-opening shirt for your own skin-to-skin
Phone charger, and coins or card for parking
The phone list and the questions note
Something to read during the quiet stretches
Cot or bassinet with a firm, well-fitting mattress
2 or 3 fitted sheets (nothing else in the cot)
Safe baby sleeping bags or swaddle wraps
Car seat, pram, and a baby carrier
Nappies, wipes, barrier cream, change mat
8 to 10 muslin cloths (they do everything)
Clothes: mostly zip suits and singlets
Baby bath or bath support, 2 soft towels
Digital thermometer, saline drops, nasal aspirator
Baby nail file or scissors
White-noise source and a dim night light
If expressing: pump, bottles, steriliser
She sleeps on her back, every sleep
Own cot or bassinet, in your room
Mattress firm and flat, sheet fitted tight
No pillows, toys, bumpers, or loose bedding in the cot
Sleeping bag correct size, or blanket tucked, feet to foot
Room comfortable, roughly 18 to 22 degrees; she is not overdressed
No cords, blinds or hanging items within reach of the cot
Home and car completely smoke-free
Swaddling stopped at first sign of rolling
Everyone who minds her knows all of the above
Evening shift agreed: you take her on arrival home
Night roster agreed and written down
Standing daily check-in call or message time set
You can solo: nappy, bath, bottle, settle, calmly
Freezer restocked for the coming month
Mum has one weekday commitment of her own booked
Mothers' group or parent group joined or scheduled
Emergency numbers saved in both phones and on the fridge
All appointments in the shared calendar
Weekend solo-parenting blocks agreed for both of you
Blue, grey or very pale lips, face or tongue
Not breathing, or pauses longer than about 20 seconds
Unresponsive, unusually floppy, or impossible to wake
A seizure or fit
A rash that does not fade when a glass is pressed on it
Choking that is silent, with no cry or cough
Under 3 months old with a temperature of 38.0 or higher, even if she seems well
Working hard to breathe: nostrils flaring, ribs sucking in, grunting with each breath
Signs of dehydration: far fewer wet nappies, dry mouth, sunken eyes or sunken fontanelle, no tears
Repeated vomiting, any green vomit, or blood in vomit or poo
Jaundice that is deepening or spreading, or a baby too sleepy to feed
A high-pitched, weak or moaning cry that is not her
She is simply not herself and your gut says something is wrong

FLAGS.t: For mum: call the midwife or emergency care
items: Soaking a maternity pad in an hour, or clots larger than a 50-cent coin
Fever, foul-smelling discharge, or a hot, red, oozing wound
Severe headache, vision changes, or sudden facial swelling
A painful, red, swollen calf, or chest pain and breathlessness (ambulance)
Feeling unable to cope, or any thought of harming herself or the baby: this is an illness, it is treatable, and it warrants a call today

QUIZ.q: For every sleep, day or night, she goes down...
QUIZ.e: Back to sleep, every time, until she can roll both ways herself. The single most protective habit in this whole module.

QUIZ.q: She is 7 weeks old with a temperature of 38.2 but seems okay. You...
QUIZ.e: Under 3 months, a fever of 38.0 or above always gets seen the same day, even in a well-looking baby.

QUIZ.q: Best everyday sign she is getting enough milk in week one?
o: Wet nappy count is on track
QUIZ.e: Roughly one wet nappy per day of age until day 5, then 5 to 6 heavy ones daily. Output is the gauge.

QUIZ.q: Choking, as opposed to gagging, is usually...
QUIZ.e: Gagging is loud, red and normal learning. A silent baby who cannot cry or cough is choking: first aid, immediately.

o: At the first sign of rolling
QUIZ.e: Any hint of rolling means arms-free sleeping bag from that day, so she can use her arms if she rolls.

QUIZ.q: Honey is off the menu until...
QUIZ.e: Infant botulism risk. No honey in any form before her first birthday.

QUIZ.q: Cluster feeding in the evenings means...
QUIZ.e: Marathon evening feeding is normal, temporary, and how she orders more milk for the days ahead.

QUIZ.q: Her crying triggers a wave of rage in you at 3am. You...
o: Put her safely in the cot and step out for 5 minutes
QUIZ.e: A crying baby in a cot is completely safe. Step out, breathe, reset, tag in the other parent if you can. Never shake a baby.
Iron stores from birth run down around 6 months. Fortified cereal, minced meat, lentils and egg lead the menu.

QUIZ.q: A newborn's comfortable awake time is about...
QUIZ.e: Including the feed. Overtired babies are harder to settle, so the clock is as useful as the yawns.

QUIZ.q: Common allergens like peanut and egg should be...
o: Introduced early, one at a time
Avoided if allergies run in the family, without advice
QUIZ.e: Current guidance: introduce early (around 6 months), one at a time, in the morning. Family history of allergy is worth a GP chat first, but delay does not protect.

QUIZ.q: What belongs in the cot with her?
o: A pillow and a soft toy
QUIZ.e: Firm mattress, fitted sheet, baby in a sleeping bag. Everything else, however cute, waits until she is much older.
